title: Research Design & the Literature Review
simple_html:
Research design is the overall plan for how you will answer your research question — the blueprint that connects your question to the data and the conclusions. The literature review is the part of your study where you survey what is already known, so you can build on it rather than reinvent it.

What design covers

  • Purpose — is the study exploratory (to scope a fuzzy problem), descriptive (to picture something accurately), or explanatory (to test cause and effect)?
  • Type of data — quantitative, qualitative, or mixed.
  • Time horizon — a snapshot (cross-sectional) or repeated over time (longitudinal).
  • Quality safeguards — how you will keep the study reliable and valid.

Why the literature review matters

  • Shows what is already known and where the gaps are.
  • Justifies your research question by pointing to a real gap.
  • Provides the theories and concepts you will use.
  • Stops you repeating work that has already been done.

How to do a literature review

Search systematically, read critically (not just summarise), group sources by theme, compare and contrast them, and end by identifying the gap your study fills.

Key takeaways

  • Design = the blueprint linking question to evidence.
  • Purpose can be exploratory, descriptive, or explanatory.
  • A literature review is critical and thematic, not a list of summaries.
  • The review's job is to justify your question by exposing a gap.
intermediate_html:
Research design is the framework that turns a research question into a project: it specifies what data you need, from whom, when, and how you will analyse it to produce credible answers. The literature review is the scholarly foundation on which the design rests — it positions your study in the existing body of knowledge and justifies why it is worth doing.

Dimensions of research design

  • Purpose: Exploratory studies clarify an ill-understood problem (often qualitative, flexible). Descriptive studies portray an accurate profile of people, events, or situations. Explanatory studies establish causal relationships between variables. Many studies combine purposes (e.g., descripto-explanatory).
  • Nature of data: quantitative, qualitative, or mixed (mono-, multi-, or mixed-method).
  • Time horizon: cross-sectional (a snapshot, the norm for time-bound dissertations) or longitudinal (repeated measurement, better for studying change).
  • Setting: field (natural) versus laboratory (controlled).
  • Quality criteria: reliability, validity, and generalisability (quantitative) or trustworthiness (qualitative), built into the design from the start.

The literature review: purpose and process

A literature review does four jobs: it maps what is known, evaluates the quality and consistency of that knowledge, identifies the gap your study addresses, and supplies the theoretical framework and concepts you will use. Crucially, it must be critical, not descriptive — it analyses, compares, and judges sources rather than merely summarising them one by one ("author A said…, author B said…").

Weak (descriptive) reviewStrong (critical) review
Summarises each source in turnSynthesises sources around themes
Accepts findings at face valueEvaluates methods, evidence, and bias
No clear argumentBuilds an argument toward a gap
Ends abruptlyEnds by stating the research gap and questions

Worked example

A student wants to study employee engagement in remote teams. The literature review reveals that engagement is well studied in co-located offices (descriptive and explanatory work abounds) but that few studies examine fully remote teams, and those that do are pre-2020 and cross-sectional. That is the gap. It justifies an explanatory, cross-sectional, quantitative design: a survey of remote employees testing whether manager communication frequency predicts engagement, controlling for tenure. The review also supplies the framework — the JD-R (Job Demands–Resources) model — from which the hypotheses are drawn. Notice how the review directly shapes purpose, data type, and even the variables.

How to apply it

  1. Search systematically: define keywords, use academic databases, record your search strategy, and apply inclusion/exclusion criteria (a mini systematic-review discipline).
  2. Read critically: note each study's method, sample, findings, and limitations.
  3. Organise thematically: group sources by concept or debate, not by author.
  4. Synthesise and critique: show where the literature agrees, conflicts, and falls silent.
  5. State the gap and derive your questions: end the review by making your study's contribution explicit.
  6. Let the review drive the design: the gap dictates purpose, data, and framework.

This lesson connects the philosophy/approach/strategy layers (Lessons 2–3) to the concrete execution layers: Sampling (Lesson 5), Data Collection & Measurement (Lesson 6), and quality assurance via Reliability, Validity & Research Ethics (Lesson 7).
full_html:
Research design is the logical structure that links a study's question to its evidence and conclusions — the "blueprint" that ensures the data collected will actually answer the question posed (de Vaus, 2001). The literature review is the scholarly substructure beneath that blueprint: it establishes what is already known, where knowledge is contested or absent, and why the proposed study merits attention. Together they translate the abstract onion layers of philosophy, approach, and strategy (Lessons 1–3) into an executable plan.

The anatomy of research design

A complete design specifies several interlocking decisions. The purpose may be exploratory (clarifying an ill-defined problem, typically flexible and qualitative), descriptive (producing an accurate profile, as in much survey work), or explanatory/causal (testing relationships between variables); Robson and McCartan (2016) add emancipatory purposes aimed at social change. The nature of data ranges across mono-method quantitative, mono-method qualitative, multi-method, and mixed methods. The time horizon is cross-sectional (a single snapshot, dominant in time-constrained dissertations) or longitudinal (repeated observation, the stronger basis for inferring change and temporal order). Setting may be a controlled laboratory or a natural field. Finally, the design must build in quality criteria appropriate to its paradigm — internal validity, external validity, construct validity, and reliability for (post)positivist designs (Campbell & Stanley, 1963), or Lincoln and Guba's (1985) trustworthiness criteria (credibility, transferability, dependability, confirmability) for interpretivist designs. The classic causal-design challenge is the trade-off between internal validity (control, favouring experiments) and external validity (realism, favouring field studies), a tension central to Cook and Campbell's (1979) work.

The functions of a literature review

A rigorous literature review performs at least five functions (Hart, 1998; Webster & Watson, 2002): it contextualises the study within a field; it synthesises prior findings into a coherent picture; it critically evaluates the strength of existing evidence and exposes methodological weaknesses; it identifies the gap — the unanswered question, contradiction, or under-studied population that the study will address; and it provides the theoretical framework that defines constructs and generates hypotheses or sensitising concepts. Webster and Watson (2002) stress that a strong review is concept-centric (organised by ideas) rather than author-centric (organised by a chronological parade of who said what). The most demanding form, the systematic literature review (Tranfield, Denyer & Smart, 2003), applies an explicit, replicable, auditable protocol — predefined search strings, databases, and inclusion/exclusion criteria — to minimise selection bias, and is increasingly expected even in narrative reviews.

Worked example

Consider a doctoral student investigating whether digital nudges reduce energy consumption in SMEs. The literature review proceeds as follows. (1) Search: she defines strings ("nudge" OR "behavioural intervention" AND "energy" AND "SME"), searches Scopus and Web of Science, and screens 480 hits down to 42 relevant studies. (2) Synthesis: she finds strong evidence that nudges work for households (Allcott, 2011) and large firms, organised around three themes — default effects, social comparison, and feedback. (3) Critique: she notes most studies are cross-sectional, US-based, and rarely measure persistence of effects. (4) Gap: almost no work tests nudges in SMEs or measures whether effects decay. (5) Framework and questions: drawing on the EAST framework and dual-process theory (Kahneman, 2011), she frames an explanatory, longitudinal, mixed-methods design: a field experiment delivering social-comparison nudges to SMEs, with follow-up interviews to explain mechanisms and a 12-month measure to test persistence. Every design decision is traceable to a specific finding or absence in the review.

Nuances and debates

First, the relationship between review and design is bidirectional in inductive work: in grounded theory the substantive literature review is sometimes deliberately delayed to avoid "forcing" data into prior categories (Glaser & Strauss, 1967), a practice contested by those who argue researchers can never be true blank slates. Second, the "gap-spotting" convention has itself been criticised: Alvesson and Sandberg (2011) argue that merely filling gaps produces incremental research and advocate problematisation — challenging the assumptions underlying existing literature — as a route to more interesting questions. Third, design is not a one-shot decision; qualitative and action-research designs are often emergent, evolving as fieldwork reveals new directions, whereas experimental designs must be fixed in advance to protect validity. Fourth, the rise of meta-analysis and systematic review means that synthesising existing evidence is now itself a respected research output, not merely a preliminary step.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • The annotated bibliography trap: a string of one-paragraph summaries with no synthesis, critique, or argument.
  • No visible gap: a review that surveys the field but never states what is missing or why the study is needed.
  • Cherry-picking: citing only supportive studies, ignoring contradictory evidence — a threat to credibility addressed by systematic search protocols.
  • Design–question mismatch: e.g., adopting a cross-sectional design then claiming to study change over time, or choosing a descriptive design for an explanatory question.
  • Confusing internal and external validity: assuming a tightly controlled lab experiment automatically generalises to the field.
  • Treating the review as static: failing to update it as the project and literature evolve.

Cross-links

Research design operationalises the philosophy and strategy chosen in Lessons 1–3 and dictates the downstream execution layers: who you study in Sampling (Lesson 5), how you measure in Data Collection & Measurement (Lesson 6), how you safeguard and report quality in Reliability, Validity & Research Ethics (Lesson 7), and how you analyse in Data Analysis (Lesson 8). A design whose every element traces back to a gap identified in the literature review is the hallmark of a coherent, defensible study.
